h1Title: Anorexia Nervosa: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Anorexia nervosa is a psychiatric and medical condition characterized by self-imposed restriction of energy intake, intense fear of gaining weight, and distorted body image, leading to significantly low body weight relative to age, sex, and developmental stage. It carries the highest mortality of any psychiatric disorder: age-standardized mortality rates are 5–10 times those of the general population, with about half of deaths from medical complications and half from suicide. Lifetime prevalence is approximately 1.4% in women and 0.2% in men; peak onset is in adolescence, typically between ages 14 and 18. Physical consequences include cardiac arrhythmias, bone density loss, electrolyte abnormalities, and multi-organ failure in severe cases. Evidence-based treatments — family-based therapy for adolescents and CBT for adults — achieve remission in 30–50% of cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Lifetime prevalence approximately 1.4% in women, 0.2% in men (Galmiche et al. Clin Nutr 2019); 12-month prevalence 0.4% in women
keyStats.demographics: 90–95% of clinical cases are female; peak onset age 14–18; increasing incidence in males, LGBTQ+ individuals, and ethnic minority populations
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of onset 16–17 years; bimodal peak at 13–14 and 17–18 years in females
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 8 million people worldwide affected (Smink et al. 2012); incidence rising particularly in higher-income countries

definition: Anorexia nervosa (ICD-10: F50.0) is an eating disorder defined by three core features: persistent energy restriction leading to significantly low body weight (below 85% of minimally expected, or BMI below 17.5 kg/m² in adults); intense fear of gaining weight or becoming fat, even when already underweight; and disturbance in the way body weight or shape is experienced, with undue influence of weight or shape on self-evaluation, or denial of the seriousness of low weight. Two subtypes are recognized: the restricting type, in which weight loss is achieved primarily through dieting, fasting, and exercise; and the binge-eating/purging type, in which the person also engages in recurrent binge eating or purging (vomiting, laxative or diuretic misuse). The ICD-10 code F50.00 specifies the unspecified variant used when subtyping is not clearly established. Anorexia nervosa is primarily managed by eating disorder specialists working in multidisciplinary teams that include psychiatry, dietetics, medicine, and nursing. The disorder produces profound medical consequences through semi-starvation: cardiac atrophy and arrhythmias from electrolyte imbalances, osteoporosis from estrogen deficiency, amenorrhea, hypophosphatemia during refeeding, and cognitive impairment from brain volume loss. It is classified in both DSM-5 (Feeding and Eating Disorders) and ICD-10/ICD-11 (Behavioural Syndromes Associated with Physiological Disturbances) as a mental disorder with major physical health consequences.

typesClassification.description: Weight loss achieved through caloric restriction, fasting, and/or excessive exercise, without binge eating or purging behavior. The most common subtype; represents approximately 70% of clinical cases.

The person engages in recurrent episodes of binge eating or purging (self-induced vomiting, misuse of laxatives, diuretics, or enemas) in the context of anorexia nervosa. Associated with higher rates of electrolyte abnormalities and impulsive behavior.

Meets all criteria for anorexia nervosa except weight is within or above normal range despite significant weight loss and anorexic cognitions. Associated with equivalent medical and psychiatric morbidity to classical anorexia.

primarySymptoms: Severely restricted caloric intake — deliberate elimination of entire food categories (fat, carbohydrate), extreme calorie counting, and refusal to eat most foods offered.
Intense, persistent fear of gaining weight or becoming fat, which does not diminish even when weight is dangerously low or body image distortion is pointed out.
Body image distortion: seeing oneself as overweight or having specific body parts as 'too fat' despite being underweight, or minimizing the seriousness of low weight.
Amenorrhea (absence of at least three consecutive menstrual cycles) in females, a consequence of hypothalamic suppression by caloric restriction and estrogen deficiency.
Excessive, compulsive exercise performed despite injury, illness, or exhaustion, driven by a need to 'compensate' for food intake or control weight.
Preoccupation with food, cooking, and nutrition — reading recipes, preparing food for others without eating, hoarding food — occupying several hours per day.
Ritual eating behaviors: cutting food into tiny pieces, eating in a fixed order, prolonged chewing, eating only from specific vessels, or eating alone.
Physical signs of starvation: lanugo (fine body hair), dry skin, brittle nails and hair, cold intolerance, constipation, and peripheral edema on refeeding.
Denial: consistently maintaining that eating and weight are 'fine', minimizing physical symptoms, and resisting medical or psychiatric assessment.
earlyWarningSigns: Expressed body dissatisfaction with increased frequency, combined with initiation of a 'diet' in an adolescent without any clinical indication for weight loss.
Socially withdrawing from mealtimes — bringing own food, eating before family meals, or excusing from shared eating under various pretexts.
New excessive exercise routine (running daily, skipping meals to exercise) combined with visible weight loss over two to four weeks.
Wearing loose clothing to conceal body shape changes, and increased anxiety or irritability when prevented from exercising or eating 'approved' foods.
Fainting, dizziness, or extreme fatigue in a young person who has recently lost visible weight.
emergencySigns: Pulse below 40 beats per minute or irregular rhythm (QTc prolongation), chest pain, or syncope — cardiac complications are the leading cause of sudden death in anorexia and require immediate inpatient monitoring.
Serum potassium below 2.5 mmol/L or phosphate below 0.5 mmol/L — life-threatening arrhythmias and refeeding syndrome risk; requires urgent intravenous correction.
BMI below 13 kg/m² with failure to sustain vital signs, or any BMI with rapid weight loss of more than 1 kg/week and signs of organ failure.
Active suicidal ideation with plan or intent — suicide accounts for approximately 25% of anorexia nervosa mortality.
Confusion, seizure, or altered consciousness during early refeeding — hypophosphatemia-driven refeeding syndrome requiring urgent metabolic management.

causes.description: Twin studies demonstrate heritability of 50–80%. Genome-wide association studies (Watson et al. Nature Genetics 2019) have identified loci related to obsessive-compulsive traits, physical activity regulation, and metabolic phenotype. Altered reward processing in the striatum, elevated serotonin 5-HT1A receptor binding, and reduced dopamine signaling contribute to food aversion and perfectionism.

causes.cause: Sociocultural pressure and internalized thin ideal
causes.description: Societies emphasizing thinness as attractive or virtuous produce higher rates of eating disorders. Exposure to social media with unattainable body-image content is associated with body dissatisfaction and dietary restriction onset in prospective studies of adolescents.

causes.cause: Psychological vulnerabilities — perfectionism, anxiety, and obsessive traits
causes.description: Perfectionism, high harm-avoidance, low impulsivity, and obsessional thinking are robust prospective risk factors. Anxiety disorders precede anorexia nervosa onset in 60–70% of cases; the food restriction and rule-following provide a sense of control over a perceived chaotic world.

Enmeshed family dynamics, high parental focus on appearance or achievement, invalidating family environments, and adverse childhood experiences (abuse, neglect, bullying) each independently increase risk. These factors interact with genetic predisposition rather than acting as primary causes.

causes.cause: Triggering events — puberty, sports culture, and life transitions
causes.description: Pubertal weight gain triggers many adolescent onset cases. Athletes in aesthetic or weight-class sports (gymnastics, ballet, rowing, wrestling) have 2–4× higher rates. Academic transitions, bereavement, and relationship loss are common precipitants in those with pre-existing vulnerability.

riskFactors.description: Women account for 90–95% of diagnosed cases; risk peaks in mid-to-late adolescence (ages 14–18). Male cases are proportionally increasing, particularly among gay and bisexual men and transgender individuals.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of eating disorder or OCD
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of people with anorexia nervosa have an 11× higher risk of the disorder; heritability estimated at 50–80% in twin studies. Familial aggregation with OCD and anxiety disorders is well-documented.
Perfectionism measured in childhood independently predicts anorexia nervosa onset in prospective studies. OCD precedes eating disorder onset in approximately 40% of clinical samples.

riskFactors.factor: Participation in weight-sensitive or aesthetic sports
riskFactors.description: Ballet, gymnastics, figure skating, rowing, wrestling, and horse-riding carry 2–4× elevated incidence rates due to cultural pressure for low weight or specific body shapes.
Sexual, physical, or emotional abuse in childhood is present in 30–50% of clinical anorexia nervosa samples; abuse history is associated with more severe illness and worse treatment outcomes.

Prospective studies in adolescents show that high frequency of social media use with body-image content independently predicts dieting behavior onset within six months, particularly in those with pre-existing body dissatisfaction.

Adolescents with type 1 diabetes have a 2× higher risk of anorexia nervosa ('diabulimia'), likely due to persistent focus on food and weight in diabetes management. The combination is associated with severe medical complications.

affectedDemographics: Females account for 90–95% of clinical cases, but rates in males are rising; male cases tend to present with muscularity focus rather than thinness and are under-diagnosed.
Adolescent girls are most commonly affected; in the UK, the National Eating Disorders Association estimates that 1 in 100 women aged 15–30 has anorexia nervosa at any time.
Elite athletes in weight-sensitive sports have incidence rates of 3–15%, compared with 0.5–1% in the general adolescent population.
LGBTQ+ individuals have significantly higher rates than heterosexual cisgender peers, particularly gay and bisexual men and transgender women.
High-income countries report higher prevalence, but the disorder is present globally; rising rates in East Asia parallel westernization of body-image norms.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis requires confirmation of the three DSM-5/ICD-10 core criteria: significantly low weight; fear of weight gain or persistent behavior that interferes with weight gain; and body image disturbance or lack of recognition of severity. Clinical assessment must include a detailed eating and exercise history, weight trajectory over six months, and assessment of cognitive features (fear of gaining weight, body checking, avoidance). Medical evaluation is essential to assess severity: CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel including potassium, phosphate, magnesium, calcium, glucose, LFTs, TFTs; 12-lead ECG for QTc and arrhythmia; bone density scan (DEXA) in patients ill for more than one year; and BMI with growth chart interpretation in adolescents. Rating scales include the Eating Disorder Examination (EDE) for structured diagnostic interview, the Eating Attitude Test-26 (EAT-26) for screening, and the Morgan-Russell Assessment Schedule for global outcome. Differential diagnoses to exclude include major depressive disorder (appetite loss without weight-gain phobia), OCD (food contamination obsessions), ARFID (avoidant-restrictive food intake disorder without body image disturbance), medical causes of weight loss (inflammatory bowel disease, hyperthyroidism, malignancy), and functional neurological symptom disorder.

diagnosticTests.test: Eating Disorder Examination (EDE), structured clinical interview
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnostic gold standard; assesses eating disorder psychopathology across four subscales (restraint, eating concern, shape concern, weight concern) and provides DSM-5/ICD-10 diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 45–60 minute semi-structured interview conducted by a trained clinician covering eating behaviors, weight history, and body image cognitions over the past 28 days. No physical component.

diagnosticTests.test: Full biochemical panel (electrolytes, phosphate, glucose, LFTs, TFTs, CBC)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantify medical severity: potassium for cardiac risk (QTc prolongation at K <3.0), phosphate for refeeding syndrome risk, TFTs to exclude hyperthyroidism as a weight-loss cause
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results usually available within 4–24 hours. Low potassium (<3.0) or phosphate (<0.8) triggers urgent medical review.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identify bradycardia, QTc prolongation, or ST/T-wave changes from electrolyte abnormalities and cardiac atrophy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A five-minute resting trace with chest and limb electrodes. QTc above 450 ms (females) or 440 ms (males) indicates elevated arrhythmia risk.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantify osteoporosis or osteopenia, which affects 50–85% of patients after one or more years of amenorrhea and low weight
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10–15 minute low-radiation scan of the spine and hip. Radiation exposure is minimal — equivalent to a few hours of background radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Brief validated screening tool for eating disorder attitudes and behaviors in clinical and community settings
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 26-item self-report questionnaire completed in five minutes. Scores above 20 indicate likely clinical eating disorder and warrant full structured assessment.

diagnosticTests.test: Resting ECG and orthostatic vital signs (lying and standing blood pressure and pulse)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detect bradycardia, orthostatic hypotension, and postural tachycardia as indicators of severe malnutrition and autonomic dysfunction
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood pressure and pulse measured lying and standing (after 2 minutes upright). Orthostatic drop of 20 mmHg systolic indicates dehydration or autonomic failure.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of anorexia nervosa is multidisciplinary and sequential, targeting medical stabilization, nutritional rehabilitation, and psychological recovery. The NICE Eating Disorders guideline (NG69, 2017) recommends family-based treatment (FBT, Maudsley method) as first-line for adolescents under 18, with evidence for 40–50% full remission at one year. For adults, specialist CBT for eating disorders (CBT-E) is first-line, with evidence supporting 25–40% remission at end-of-treatment; the Enhanced CBT (Fairburn) protocol is the most widely used adaptation. Specialist supportive clinical management (SSCM) is an alternative with comparable outcomes. Nutritional rehabilitation — structured meal plans targeting 0.5–1 kg/week weight gain in outpatients and 1 kg/week in inpatients — is necessary before meaningful psychological work can occur; severe cognitive impairment from starvation prevents engagement with therapy until weight is restored. Medical admission is required when BMI falls below 13–14 kg/m², when vital signs are unstable, when electrolytes are critically low, or when medical monitoring during refeeding syndrome prevention is required (MARSIPAN guidelines). Refeeding syndrome, characterized by severe hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, and hypomagnesemia during rapid refeeding, is prevented by low initial caloric load (1,000–1,200 kcal/day) with electrolyte monitoring and replacement. Nasogastric feeding under legal authorization may be required in severe or life-threatening cases. Pharmacotherapy has limited evidence: no medication has regulatory approval for anorexia nervosa. SSRIs do not produce weight gain in underweight patients. Olanzapine (2.5–10 mg/day) shows modest benefit in weight gain and anxiety reduction in some RCTs but is not first-line. Bone protection with calcium and vitamin D supplementation is indicated for patients with osteoporosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Family-based treatment (FBT/Maudsley method), 20 sessions over 12 months
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for adolescents (under 18). Parents take control of refeeding in phase one, then gradually return control to the adolescent in phases two and three. Delivered by a trained therapist with family sessions. Requires motivated family and therapist trained in the protocol.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Full remission in 40–50% of adolescents at end-of-treatment; 67–80% at 4-year follow-up (Lock et al. AACAP 2010)

medicalTreatments.name: CBT-E (Enhanced CBT for eating disorders), 40 sessions over 40 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Structured individual therapy targeting eating-disorder-specific cognitions: overvaluation of shape and weight, dietary restraint, and events/mood maintaining restriction. Addresses both restricting and binge-purge presentations. Requires adequate cognitive function (BMI generally above 15).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Remission in 30–40% of adults at end of treatment; comparable to other leading psychological therapies in head-to-head trials (Fairburn et al. 2015)

medicalTreatments.name: Specialist supportive clinical management (SSCM), 20 sessions
medicalTreatments.description: Combines clinical management (psychoeducation, normalization of eating) with supportive psychotherapy. Effective for patients unable to engage with full CBT-E due to low weight or motivation. Shows efficacy comparable to CBT-E in Australian RCTs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable remission rates to CBT-E in several trials; may be better tolerated in severely ill patients with limited insight

medicalTreatments.name: Nutritional rehabilitation with structured meal support
medicalTreatments.description: Individualized meal plan targeting 0.5–1.0 kg/week outpatient weight gain. Provided by a specialist dietitian with meal support by trained staff or family. Phosphate, potassium, and magnesium monitoring two to three times weekly during initial refeeding.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Weight restoration to BMI above 19 is associated with 50% reduction in mortality risk; every 1 BMI unit increase in first 6 months predicts better outcome

medicalTreatments.name: Olanzapine (2.5–10 mg/day at night)
medicalTreatments.description: The only medication with RCT evidence for modest weight gain and reduction in obsessional preoccupation with food in anorexia nervosa. Not first-line; used as an adjunct when weight gain is inadequate on psychological treatment alone. Monitor for metabolic syndrome.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest additional weight gain (0.25–0.5 BMI units at 8 weeks) in meta-analysis vs placebo; effect significant but small (Dold et al. 2015)

medicalTreatments.name: Nasogastric tube feeding (under MARSIPAN guidance or legal authorization)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in life-threatening cases when oral intake is insufficient and the patient cannot consent to or refuses essential refeeding. Initiated under mental health legislation in most jurisdictions. Low initial rate (10–20 ml/hour) with stepwise increase over 7–10 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Life-saving in critically ill patients; associated with weight restoration to safe levels in 80–90% of initiated cases in specialist units

alternativeTreatments.name: Acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Emerging evidence for ACT in anorexia nervosa targeting psychological inflexibility about food and body; particularly for treatment-resistant patients and as an adjunct to CBT-E.

alternativeTreatments.name: Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS)
alternativeTreatments.description: Open-label studies suggest rTMS to dorsolateral prefrontal cortex reduces urge to restrict and food-related anxiety in anorexia nervosa; under active investigation in RCTs.

whySeeSpecialist: Anorexia nervosa has the highest mortality of any psychiatric disorder and requires management by a multidisciplinary eating disorder team. Primary care practitioners should refer promptly when BMI falls below 17.5 kg/m², when weight loss exceeds 1 kg/week, when cardiac or electrolyte abnormalities are detected, or when functional impairment is significant. Delays in specialist referral increase illness chronicity and mortality risk.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an eating disorder specialist team including a psychiatrist, clinical psychologist trained in FBT or CBT-E, specialist dietitian, and liaison physician. In the UK, NHS eating disorder services and private BEAT-registered providers are appropriate pathways. In the US, seek CEDS-certified therapists (Certified Eating Disorder Specialist) and programs accredited by the Alliance for Eating Disorders Awareness.
hospitalCriteria: BMI below 13–14 kg/m² in adults, or below the 2nd percentile in children, with medical instability
Bradycardia below 40 bpm, QTc above 500 ms, or hemodynamically significant hypotension
Serum potassium below 2.5 mmol/L or phosphate below 0.5 mmol/L
Failure to gain weight or continued weight loss despite optimal outpatient treatment
Active suicidal intent or severe co-occurring psychiatric disorder requiring inpatient management
Refeeding syndrome prevention required under medical supervision
keyFacilities: Dedicated eating disorder inpatient units with specialist medical-psychiatric teams
Intensive eating disorder outpatient programs (3–5 days/week)
Day hospital programs for step-up or step-down care
MARSIPAN-compliant medical wards for medically critical patients
Community eating disorder teams for outpatient management
preventionStrategies: Implement school-based body-image and media-literacy programs for adolescents; randomized trials show 40–50% reduction in eating disorder risk attitudes.
Train coaches in weight-sensitive sports to identify early warning signs and avoid weight-loss pressure on young athletes.
Promote weight-neutral health messaging in primary care to avoid triggering restrictive eating in those with perfectionist or anxious temperament.
Screen adolescents with type 1 diabetes, OCD, or anxiety disorder annually using the EAT-26 given their 2–4× elevated eating disorder risk.
Reduce social media exposure to appearance-focused content and promote positive body image through peer-based programs with documented efficacy (e.g., the Body Project).
lifestyleModifications: Follow the prescribed meal plan with full caloric targets — do not substitute 'healthier' alternatives that reduce energy density without dietitian approval.
Rest from all competitive or excessive exercise until weight is restored to BMI above 18.5 and with medical clearance; gentle walking is acceptable during recovery.
Practice one planned 'exposure meal' weekly that includes a previously feared food, progressing through the hierarchy developed with the dietitian.
Attend all meal support sessions and family mealtimes; eating with others consistently improves outcomes compared with solitary eating.
Limit body checking (weighing more than once per week, measuring body parts, mirror scrutiny) using behavioral targets agreed with the treatment team.
Identify and communicate social and emotional triggers for restriction to the treatment team so these can be addressed in therapy.

recommended: Structured meal and snack plan prescribed by an eating disorder dietitian, including all macronutrients (carbohydrate, protein, fat) without elimination
Adequate dietary phosphate, potassium, and magnesium prior to and during refeeding to minimize refeeding syndrome risk
Calcium (1,000–1,500 mg/day) and vitamin D (800–2,000 IU/day) supplementation to address bone density loss
Energy-dense foods normalized progressively during weight restoration rather than relying solely on high-volume low-density foods
avoid: Dietary rules, 'clean eating' programs, or elimination of entire food groups during recovery — these perpetuate restrictive cognitions
Excessive fluid intake to create false sense of fullness or manipulate weigh-in results
Energy expenditure via exercise at volumes inconsistent with the prescribed meal plan during weight restoration
Diet apps and calorie-counting tools, which reinforce the food preoccupation maintaining the disorder

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise restriction is a core medical recommendation during active illness at low weight. Compulsive exercise must be stopped or strictly supervised. When BMI reaches 18.5 and medical clearance is given, gentle activity (30-minute walks, yoga) can resume. Return to sport or structured exercise follows a graded protocol agreed with the treatment team, conditioning return on sustained weight maintenance and absence of compensatory use.
dailyManagement: Eat all planned meals and snacks at scheduled times, including evening snacks, without substitution.
Weigh yourself no more than once per week (or as directed by the treatment team) in the same conditions to track progress without reinforcing weight preoccupation.
Attend all medical monitoring appointments for electrolytes and vital signs, particularly in the first six weeks of refeeding.
Use prescribed oral phosphate supplements if directed; take with food to minimize gastrointestinal side effects.
Communicate openly with the treatment team about episodes of restriction, purging, or excessive exercise — honesty enables treatment adjustment.
Engage in designated rest periods after meals to reduce compensatory exercise urge, using distraction (reading, conversation, music) for at least 30 minutes post-meal.
prognosis: Anorexia nervosa has the highest mortality of any psychiatric disorder, with all-cause mortality approximately 5–10% per decade of illness and standardized mortality ratios of 5–10 compared with the general population (Arcelus et al. Archives Gen Psychiatry 2011). Approximately half of deaths are from medical complications and half from suicide. However, the long-term outcome is not uniformly poor: a systematic review found full recovery in 46% of patients, partial recovery in 33%, and chronic illness in 21% at 5–10 year follow-up. Recovery is more likely with early treatment (within 3 years of onset), younger age of onset, absence of purging behavior, minimal comorbid psychiatric illness, and good family support. Bone density loss may be permanent even after weight restoration; osteoporosis-related fractures are a significant long-term complication. Fertility is usually restored with weight restoration and resumption of menstruation.
recoveryTimeline: Medical stabilization occurs within one to four weeks of consistent refeeding. Weight restoration to BMI 18.5+ typically takes three to six months with structured treatment. Psychological recovery (reduction in eating disorder cognitions, flexible food attitudes) lags physical recovery by 6–18 months. Relapse rates are high in the first two years (30–50%) and require regular monitoring. Full sustained recovery, including psychological normalization, takes an average of five to seven years from illness onset.
complications: Cardiac complications: bradycardia (HR <60 in 80% of hospitalized patients), QTc prolongation, pericardial effusion, and sudden cardiac death from ventricular arrhythmia — the leading medical cause of mortality.

Osteoporosis: bone density loss occurs at 2–4% per year of illness and affects 85% of patients after amenorrhea lasting more than one year; stress fractures and early-onset osteoporotic fractures are long-term consequences.

Refeeding syndrome: hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, and hypomagnesemia occurring when carbohydrate reintroduction shifts phosphate intracellularly; cardiac failure and seizure risk in first 2–5 days of refeeding.
Hypokalemia from purging behavior: potassium below 2.5 mmol/L causes muscle weakness, ileus, and potentially fatal cardiac arrhythmias.
Brain volume loss: structural MRI studies show grey and white matter volume reduction associated with cognitive impairment; partially reversible with weight restoration.
Comorbid psychiatric disorders: depression (50–80%), OCD (40%), anxiety disorders (60%), and suicidal ideation (25%), all requiring parallel management.

supportResources.name: Beat Eating Disorders (UK)
supportResources.description: UK's leading eating disorder charity; provides helpline, online support groups, and treatment finder for specialist services.

supportResources.name: National Eating Disorders Association (NEDA) — USA
supportResources.description: Helpline, free screening tools, and treatment provider directory for anorexia nervosa and all eating disorders.

supportResources.name: FEAST — Families Empowered and Supporting Treatment of Eating Disorders
supportResources.description: International organization supporting parents and caregivers using evidence-based approaches; particularly relevant for adolescent anorexia.

supportResources.name: Royal College of Psychiatrists — Eating Disorders
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on anorexia nervosa, diagnosis, and treatment options from the UK's professional psychiatric body.

confusedWithConditions.name: Major depressive disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Major depressive disorder produces appetite loss and weight loss but does not involve fear of weight gain, overvaluation of thinness, or body image disturbance. A depressed patient wishes they could eat but cannot; a patient with anorexia nervosa does not want to gain weight. The two frequently co-occur and require parallel treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Obsessive-compulsive disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: OCD may involve food contamination obsessions or symmetry rituals that affect eating, but the central fear is contamination or harm, not weight gain. Body image disturbance and overvaluation of thinness are absent in pure OCD. OCD and anorexia nervosa co-occur in approximately 40% of cases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Adjustment disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Adjustment disorder with depressed mood may reduce appetite and cause weight loss as a secondary symptom, but it lacks the weight-gain phobia, body image disturbance, and chronic food restriction that define anorexia nervosa. It is temporally linked to a stressor and resolves with stressor resolution.

confusedWithConditions.name: Opioid use disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Opioid use disorder causes weight loss through appetite suppression, but weight loss is a side effect rather than a goal. The individual does not exhibit fear of weight gain or body image disturbance. However, substance use disorders co-occur with anorexia nervosa, particularly the binge-purge subtype, and must be screened for.

relatedConditions.name: Obsessive-compulsive disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares neurobiological features (serotonin dysregulation, compulsive behavior patterns) and co-occurs in approximately 40% of cases; OCD frequently precedes anorexia nervosa onset.

relatedConditions.name: Major depressive disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Present in 50–80% of anorexia nervosa cases; depression may precede, accompany, or result from starvation and requires parallel treatment for full recovery.

relatedConditions.name: Generalized anxiety disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Anxiety disorder precedes anorexia nervosa in 60–70% of cases and is a major driver of food restriction as a control mechanism.

relatedConditions.name: Bipolar disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Bipolar disorder co-occurs in approximately 10% of eating disorder patients; the binge-purge subtype of anorexia overlaps with manic impulsivity.

relatedConditions.name: Agoraphobia
relatedConditions.relevance: Social avoidance driven by body image concerns in anorexia nervosa can overlap with agoraphobic restriction; both involve progressive behavioral avoidance patterns.

faqs.question: What is anorexia nervosa?
faqs.answer: Anorexia nervosa is an eating disorder characterized by deliberate food restriction, intense fear of gaining weight, and distorted body image, leading to dangerously low body weight. It has the highest mortality of any psychiatric disorder — about 5–10% per decade. It affects roughly 1.4% of women and 0.2% of men over a lifetime.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of anorexia nervosa?
faqs.answer: Warning signs include sudden dieting without clinical reason, avoiding mealtimes, excessive exercise, wearing loose clothes to hide weight loss, extreme food restriction, and expressing intense fear of gaining weight. Fainting, fatigue, and amenorrhea are early physical signs. Early intervention significantly improves outcomes.

faqs.question: What causes anorexia nervosa?
faqs.answer: Anorexia nervosa results from an interaction of genetic vulnerability (heritability 50–80%), psychological traits (perfectionism, high anxiety), sociocultural pressure toward thinness, and triggering events (puberty, life transitions, sports culture). No single cause exists; it is a biopsychosocial disorder requiring multidisciplinary treatment.

faqs.question: Is anorexia nervosa life-threatening?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anorexia nervosa has the highest mortality of any psychiatric disorder. Standardized mortality ratios are 5–10 times the general population; half of deaths are from cardiac complications and half from suicide. Bradycardia, electrolyte abnormalities, and bone loss are the major medical risks.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for anorexia nervosa?
faqs.answer: For adolescents, family-based treatment (Maudsley method) achieves full remission in 40–50% at one year. For adults, Enhanced CBT (CBT-E) is first-line. Nutritional rehabilitation is essential before psychological work. Inpatient treatment is needed when BMI falls below 13–14 or vital signs are unstable.

faqs.question: Can anorexia nervosa be cured?
faqs.answer: Full recovery is achieved in approximately 46% of patients over 5–10 years; 33% achieve partial recovery. Early treatment, adolescent onset, and absence of purging behavior predict better outcomes. Complete psychological recovery typically takes 5–7 years. Relapse in the first two years is common (30–50%) and treatable.

faqs.question: What is refeeding syndrome?
faqs.answer: Refeeding syndrome is a potentially fatal metabolic complication occurring in severely malnourished patients during early nutritional rehabilitation. Reintroducing carbohydrate triggers a shift of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium into cells, causing critically low serum levels with cardiac, neurological, and respiratory complications.

faqs.question: Does anorexia nervosa cause permanent bone damage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Estrogen deficiency from amenorrhea and inadequate calcium intake cause bone density loss at 2–4% per year. Osteoporosis develops in 85% of patients after a year or more of illness. Some bone loss persists even after weight restoration and menstrual recovery, making early treatment important for long-term bone health.

faqs.question: Can men get anorexia nervosa?
faqs.answer: Yes. Men account for 5–10% of anorexia nervosa cases, and rates are rising. Male presentations often involve a drive for muscularity rather than extreme thinness, which delays diagnosis. Gay and bisexual men have higher rates than heterosexual men. Medical complications are identical to female patients.

faqs.question: What medications are used in anorexia nervosa?
faqs.answer: No medication has regulatory approval specifically for anorexia nervosa. Olanzapine (2.5–10 mg/day) shows modest benefit for weight gain and food preoccupation in some trials. Calcium and vitamin D are prescribed for bone protection. SSRIs do not produce weight gain in underweight patients and are not first-line.

faqs.question: At what BMI is hospitalization required for anorexia nervosa?
faqs.answer: Hospital admission is typically required when BMI falls below 13–14 kg/m² in adults, when vital signs are unstable (heart rate below 40, systolic BP below 80), when electrolytes are critically abnormal, or when outpatient weight restoration has failed. MARSIPAN guidelines are the UK standard for medical risk stratification.

faqs.question: Is anorexia nervosa genetic?
faqs.answer: Genetic factors account for approximately 50–80% of risk, based on twin studies. First-degree relatives have an 11× higher risk. A 2019 genome-wide study identified loci associated with obsessive-compulsive traits, physical activity, and metabolic phenotype. Genetics interacts with environmental and psychological factors.

faqs.question: Can anorexia nervosa cause heart problems?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cardiac complications are the leading medical cause of death. Semi-starvation causes cardiac atrophy, bradycardia (heart rate below 40 in severe cases), QTc prolongation, and pericardial effusion. Electrolyte abnormalities (particularly low potassium from purging) cause ventricular arrhythmias.

faqs.question: How does family-based treatment work for anorexia nervosa?
faqs.answer: Family-based treatment (Maudsley method) involves parents taking control of refeeding in a non-blaming way in phase one, then gradually returning autonomy to the adolescent in phase two, and addressing development in phase three. It is delivered in 20 sessions over 12 months and achieves remission in 40–50% of adolescents.

faqs.question: Does anorexia nervosa affect fertility?
faqs.answer: Active anorexia nervosa suppresses ovulation and causes amenorrhea through hypothalamic dysfunction, making conception unlikely during illness. Fertility typically returns within six months of weight restoration and menstrual resumption. However, pregnancy during active anorexia carries significant maternal and fetal risks.

metaTitle: Anorexia Nervosa: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Anorexia nervosa: food restriction and intense fear of weight gain, with the highest psychiatric mortality. Symptoms, health risks, and treatment reviewed.
